exercises.description: Create a Product class with class attributes (currency='USD', tax_rate=0.08) and instance attributes (name, price). Add a method to calculate the after-tax price.

    # TODO: Add class attribute: tax_rate = 0.08

Coffee Mug: $12.99 USD (with tax: $14.03)
Tax rate: 0.08

Access class attribute as Product.tax_rate or self.tax_rate

exercises.title: Instance Counting with Class Attributes

exercises.title: Object Identity vs Equality
exercises.description: Create multiple Car instances and demonstrate the difference between identity (is) and equality (==). Also show how instance attributes shadow class attributes.
exercises.starterCode:
class Car:
    fuel_type = "gasoline"
    wheels = 4


# TODO: Create two separate Car instances: car1 and car2

# TODO: Set car1.model = 'Camry' and car2.model = 'Civic'

# TODO: Print whether car1 is car2 (should be False)

# TODO: Create car3 = car1 (alias, same object)
# Print whether car1 is car3 (should be True)

# TODO: Show instance attribute shadowing class attribute:
# Set car1.fuel_type = 'electric'
# Print car1.fuel_type (should be 'electric' - instance attribute)
# Print car2.fuel_type (should be 'gasoline' - still class attribute)
exercises.expectedOutput:
car1 is car2: False
car1 is car3: True
car1 fuel: electric
car2 fuel: gasoline
hints: car1 = Car(); car2 = Car() creates two independent instances
car3 = car1 makes car3 an alias (same object in memory)
car1 is car2 compares identity (memory address)
Setting car1.fuel_type = 'electric' creates an instance attribute that shadows the class one

warmup.title: Quick Recall: isinstance and type
warmup.description: What does this code print? Think about how isinstance differs from type().
warmup.starterCode:
class Animal:
    pass

class Dog(Animal):
    pass

fido = Dog()
print(type(fido))
print(isinstance(fido, Dog))
print(isinstance(fido, Animal))
print(type(fido) == Animal)
